content:

Might this be related to the recently merged rust: block: introduce kernel::block::mq module?

Just want to mentioned it here, so sorry if its unrelated :wink:

I haven't done any staring at lockdep traces, so I am very unfamiliar with this. If I read it correctly, this is a problem in the call to __blk_mq_alloc_disk called from line 109 in rust/kernel/block/mq/gen_disk.rs. I don't see anything obviously wrong with that call, but I am very unsure. Does this also happen with v6.11-rc2?
cc @Andreas Hindborg
Hmm, I am not seeing this with the tests on x86. I'll try to see if I can hit it on arm64
Is this a clean v6.11-rc1 or do you have things on top?
And also, can I possibly have your config?

Andreas Hindborg said:

Is this a clean v6.11-rc1 or do you have things on top?

It's rust-dev heavily patched with the SoC support.

can I possibly have your config?

Yes, I'll send it.

Benno Lossin said:

Does this also happen with v6.11-rc2?

Yes:

...

Does it reproduce in upstream rc2 or only in your patched version?
I still did not get to do an arm build, hence all the questions. I’ll try to get it done today

Andreas Hindborg said:

Does it reproduce in upstream rc2 or only in your patched version?

Only the patched version (required to run on the target).
Ah, I see
So this trace is from real hardware, not qemu?

Andreas Hindborg said:

So this trace is from real hardware, not qemu?

Yes :grinning:

I was unclear regarding the

BUG: key ffff800083a5bce8 has not been registered!

and therefore put that into Google. And got **[next] [arm64] [gpio] BUG: key has not been registered! DEBUG_LOCKS_WARN_ON**

I need to read that carefully to see if that helps :wink:
It’s complaining that the lock ID is unknown to lockdep. Which is strange, because it should be transparent and happen automatically.
Or, that is my guess anyway
Could you give me a pointer to instructions on cross compiling for arm64?
Do I just get a clang for arm64, a rustc for arm64 and then go ARCH=arm64 and that's it?

Andreas Hindborg said:

Could you give me a pointer to instructions on cross compiling for arm64?

export CROSS_COMPILE=aarch64-linux-gnu-; export ARCH=arm64
make distclean; make <target>_defconfig LLVM=1; make -j8 LLVM=1

Not sure if the CROSS_COMPILE is reqiured in LLVM context, though :wink:
Thanks

Benno Lossin said:

I haven't done any staring at lockdep traces, so I am very unfamiliar with this. If I read it correctly, this is a problem in the call to __blk_mq_alloc_disk called from line 109 in rust/kernel/block/mq/gen_disk.rs. I don't see anything obviously wrong with that call, but I am very unsure.

Is it allowed to pass a NULL pointer (core::ptr::null_mut()) for void *queuedata? At least this looks special in this case compared to other calls of __blk_mq_alloc_disk(). Sorry, just comparing this with other calls of __blk_mq_alloc_disk() :wink:

       // SAFETY: `tagset.raw_tag_set()` points to a valid and initialized tag set
        let gendisk = from_err_ptr(unsafe {
            bindings::__blk_mq_alloc_disk(
                tagset.raw_tag_set(),
                &mut lim,
                core::ptr::null_mut(),
                lock_class_key.as_ptr(),
            )
        })?;
Yes, that is unrelated to the lock class key
It is a pointer to the per io-queue private data for the driver. We set it to null as we are not supporting that feature yet. It should be OK.
I managed to build for arm64. Now I just have to figure out how to run it on qemu.

OK, I was able to build and test en qemu, using the builtin kunit tooling. Basically 

make Image.gz
tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py exec --make_options LLVM=1 --build_dir <out-dir> --arch=arm64

That runs all the tests and they all pass:

...
[14:55:42] [PASSED] rust_doctest_kernel_block_mq_rs_0
...
But this is on a upstream 6.11-rc2 with my own configuration. I did not try your patches or config yet.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Hmm, I am not seeing this with the tests on x86

Andreas Hindborg said:

upstream 6.11-rc2 with my own configuration

I'm unclear if these tests had CONFIG_LOCKDEP enabled?

Anyhow, doing the registration manually seems to "fix" the issue in my setup:

diff --git a/rust/kernel/block/mq/gen_disk.rs b/rust/kernel/block/mq/gen_disk.rs
index f548a61998477..503b183c329db 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/block/mq/gen_disk.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/block/mq/gen_disk.rs
@@ -94,6 +94,7 @@ pub fn build<T: Operations>(
         tagset: Arc<TagSet<T>>,
     ) -> Result<GenDisk<T>> {
         let lock_class_key = crate::sync::LockClassKey::new();
+        crate::sync::LockClassKey::register_key(lock_class_key.as_ptr());

         // SAFETY: `bindings::queue_limits` contain only fields that are valid when zeroed.
         let mut lim: bindings::queue_limits = unsafe { core::mem::zeroed() };
diff --git a/rust/kernel/sync.rs b/rust/kernel/sync.rs
index 81d1830f194b2..e88fe55f7afb7 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/sync.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/sync.rs
@@ -39,6 +39,12 @@ pub const fn new() -> Self {
     pub(crate) fn as_ptr(&self) -> *mut bindings::lock_class_key {
         self.0.get()
     }
+
+    #[allow(unused_variables)]
+    pub(crate) fn register_key(key: *mut bindings::lock_class_key) {
+        #[cfg(CONFIG_LOCKDEP)]
+        unsafe { bindings::lockdep_register_key(key) };
+    }
 }

 impl Default for LockClassKey {

Dirk Behme said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Hmm, I am not seeing this with the tests on x86

Andreas Hindborg said:

upstream 6.11-rc2 with my own configuration

I'm unclear if these tests had CONFIG_LOCKDEP enabled?

I thought I did, but since you asked I went back and checked, and it seems I did not (only for x86). So I will just rebuild and rerun with lockdep.
Not able to reproduce on upstream on arm64 with lockdep enabled.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Not able to reproduce on upstream on arm64 with lockdep enabled.

Thanks for confirming! Strange ...
Still, I am running on qemu, not real HW.
Hmm, maybe I need to enable debug_locks at runtime?
I only added the kconfig option. Do I need a command line option as well?
No, it seems to be default enabled.

Andreas Hindborg said:

I only added the kconfig option. Do I need a command line option as well?

Not that I'm aware ...

This code in lockdep.c is making the warning:

    if (!static_obj(key) && !is_dynamic_key(key)) {
        if (debug_locks)
            printk(KERN_ERR "BUG: key %px has not been registered!\n", key);
        DEBUG_LOCKS_WARN_ON(1);
        return;
    }
OK, I managed to reproduce. KUnit was reporting the test as successful, but looking at the raw kernel output, I see the same error you reported.

Well, that is unfortunate:

    # rust_doctest_kernel_block_mq_rs_0.location: rust/kernel/block/mq.rs:58
BUG: key fff00000017dfcf8 has not been registered!

Apparently a BUG does not cause KUnit to flag an error.
I'll check if I made the same mistake on x86.

Besides the patch I sent above I was initially thinking about doing the registration directly in LockClassKey::new() but couldn't figure out the correct syntax. I think in the end it complained about the const somewhere (?). Something like this:

diff --git a/rust/kernel/sync.rs b/rust/kernel/sync.rs
index e88fe55f7afb7..04550954af3b0 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/sync.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/sync.rs
@@ -33,7 +33,10 @@ unsafe impl Sync for LockClassKey {}
 impl LockClassKey {
     /// Creates a new lock class key.
     pub const fn new() -> Self {
-        Self(Opaque::uninit())
+        let key = Self(Opaque::uninit());
+        #[cfg(CONFIG_LOCKDEP)]
+        unsafe { bindings::lockdep_register_key(key.0.get()) };
+        key
     }

     pub(crate) fn as_ptr(&self) -> *mut bindings::lock_class_key {
You can't do it there. key is moved after you call lockdep_register_key, so the address is wrong.
Should the key be pinned after registration?
Since registration is using the address

Dirk Behme said:

Besides the patch I sent above I was initially thinking about doing the registration directly in LockClassKey::new() but couldn't figure out the correct syntax. I think in the end it complained about the const somewhere (?). Something like this:

diff --git a/rust/kernel/sync.rs b/rust/kernel/sync.rs
index e88fe55f7afb7..04550954af3b0 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/sync.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/sync.rs
@@ -33,7 +33,10 @@ unsafe impl Sync for LockClassKey {}
 impl LockClassKey {
     /// Creates a new lock class key.
     pub const fn new() -> Self {
-        Self(Opaque::uninit())
+        let key = Self(Opaque::uninit());
+        #[cfg(CONFIG_LOCKDEP)]
+        unsafe { bindings::lockdep_register_key(key.0.get()) };
+        key
     }

     pub(crate) fn as_ptr(&self) -> *mut bindings::lock_class_key {

I think the correct thing to do is to call lockdep_register_key no matter what. Perhaps we should add a new_registered method that returns a Pin<Box<LockClassKey>>?
Also, the bug is happening on x86, I just failed to see it because KUnit does not fail the test even though there is a BUG.
Are you not using static_lock_class?

Alice Ryhl said:

Are you not using static_lock_class?

No, I am using a dynamic class key
It is a per-instance lock, so I would assume I need a class per instance.
The number of instances is dynamic.
You usually don't need that.
I skimmed locking docs, but did not find anything. Could you elaborate?
So, the way lockdep works is that every time you take more than one lock at the time, it records their class and which order you took them in.
E.g. if you have mutex A with class AC and mutex B with class BC, then locking A and then B at the same time records AC,BC.
If you later lock B and then A, then it records BC,AC.
This triggers a lockdep error because AC,BC and BC,AC have the opposite ordering.
And you must always lock things in the same order to avoid deadlocks.
Got it, thanks.
If A1 and A2 both have class AC, then locking A1 and A2 at the same time also always results in an error.
Since they have the same class.
You can reuse lockdep classes as long as none of the above cases results in lockdep warnings.

Alice Ryhl said:

If A1 and A2 both have class AC, then locking A1 and A2 at the same time also always results in an error.

I'm reading in docs that all inode locks have same class. Combining that with what you are writing would mean that a kernel thread can only ever hold one inode lock. Is that correct?
Yes. Or at least, to lock more than one you would need to do something special.
Anyways, it would be a bug for at thread to hold locks for multiple devices at the same time, so a static should be fine.
@Dirk Behme do you want to submit a patch, changing the lock class to static?
For example, using try_lock can safely lock things with the same class, or with classes in the wrong order, since try_lock doesn't sleep and can't result in deadlocks.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Dirk Behme do you want to submit a patch, changing the lock class to static?

Feel free to do it correctly :wink: If you like, you could add my Reported-by :smiley: I can give it a tested by, then :big_smile:

And most probably we need a Fixes tag and include it as fix in this -rc cycle.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Apparently a BUG does not cause KUnit to flag an error.

Wait really?! That sounds unintentional...
Oh but in the code above, there is no BUG call, it is only printing BUG

Benno Lossin said:

Oh but in the code above, there is no BUG call, it is only printing BUG

Right. That is also confusing.

Hmm, I am getting a modpost error for the rnull module when I change to a static key:

ERROR: modpost: "_RNvNvMs_NtNtNtCs1cdwasc6FUb_6kernel5block2mq8gen_diskNtB6_14GenDiskBuilder5build5CLASS" [drivers/block/rnull_mod.ko] undefined!
Building the module as built-in is fine.

I have the symbol in the kernel:

objdump -t linux-build/vmlinux | rustfilt | grep GenDisk | grep CLASS
ffffffff82b93cd0 g     O .bss   0000000000000010 <kernel::block::mq::gen_disk::GenDiskBuilder>::build::CLASS

This seems to be caused by symbols in the bss segment not being exported. It is fixed by:

modified   rust/Makefile
@@ -305,7 +305,7 @@ $(obj)/bindings/bindings_helpers_generated.rs: $(src)/helpers.c FORCE
 quiet_cmd_exports = EXPORTS $@
       cmd_exports = \
    $(NM) -p --defined-only $< \
-       | awk '/ (T|R|D) / {printf "EXPORT_SYMBOL_RUST_GPL(%s);\n",$$3}' > $@
+       | awk '/ (T|R|D|B) / {printf "EXPORT_SYMBOL_RUST_GPL(%s);\n",$$3}' > $@

 $(obj)/exports_core_generated.h: $(obj)/core.o FORCE
    $(call if_changed,exports)

Any build system experts around for this? @Miguel Ojeda
I guess this would be a problem for binder as well, if it is linked as a module @Alice Ryhl ?
https://r.android.com/3112324
Seems that way. I was not aware of this issue, though.
Maybe you export kernel symbols in a different way in Android?
What do you mean?

I mean that when you build binder in the context of android, maybe you are not using the awk snippet above to generate the list of exported symbols? Maybe you are not even using modpost? I have no idea how different android build system is from upstream kbuild.
But really, anyone using an abstraction that instantiates a lock would be hit by this problem.
If you look at the link I shared, it makes the same change to awk.
@Dirk Behme https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20240815074519.2684107-3-nmi@metaspace.dk/
Thanks a lot for finding this!
Andreas Hindborg has marked this topic as resolved.
